author: @captainmidnite93
text: To loosen metals w/o scary flame a carbon rod (this roadie used old Fashioned spotlight rods) in a welder dialed up from zero is gentler. And of course, whack the middle while it's still colder.

author: @steveharrison7615
text: Bit of heat , always the first option

author: @craigglewis
text: LOL Too Funny. It's what I had to do just last week. I was pounding away and thought. Heat it up. It Works AMAZING LOL :)

author: @Gwydion67
text:
Robert, why didn't you ask before? 😉
I would have told you, since a very similar method (the arch of an e-welder) worked out for me back in the nineties. 💥👍

author: @karlmyers6518
text: Heat is always a good friend when I work on cars or a cheap rattle gun on bolts

author: @carstenweiland7896
text: Get a puller, these work so fine and are probably 2 pounds, got mine for ages!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the only ones I saw were a lot more than 2 pound lol

replies.author: @carstenweiland7896
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Can send you one free of charge if you like (Not a shop or anything, I would send you my old one that is still working), should I send a contact request for the address on your FWG webside? Got no Address!

replies.author: @carstenweiland7896
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Bike Tool is in the Mail and incoming!

author: @johndee68
text: No safety glasses, for in the case of any metal shattering.

replies.author: @johndee68
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering sort of, considering there could be people watching who aren't aware of such possible effects, it couldn't hurt to advise such people of a little safety. 
I am also curious, are threads on the cranks ever damaged by using the bolt and hammer method?

author: @timothygorman7528
text:
I'm laughing right now, because after you had explained you had struggled with it for over 45 minutes "This occurred to me" - and you cut to the video clip with the torch, your facial expression shows your frustration.  You holding the torch and your physical demeanor just says it all, lol.

If it didn't work, I imagine you'd have wanted to take that torch and melt the bike down into a liquid pile of steel or aluminum, ha ha.

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text: Common technique in metal industry, bearings are heated in an oven above 200C and shrunk on a cold shaft. To remove the old bearings they need to be heated without heating up the inner shaft. Therefor a ring with pressurized air is used to cool down the shaft.

author: @staycurious8650
text: Be careful not to overheat, the inner shaft will also heat up and expand.

author: @12thsonofisrael
text:
That's what I'm talking about heat expansion. 

And, when you have cause to reinsert the shaft into the crank,
Place the shaft into the deep freeze for an hour, then warm up the crank, remove the shaft from the deep freeze, dip the end of the shaft into 30 weight engine oil, press on the crank and, you're done.

🤓

author: @CitizenJourno-1
text: Wow... Heat is cool if you're stuck

text: Another way to separate taper fit is to back one side with a sledge or anyting solid an then smack the opposing side

author: @stevetobias4890
text: lol, I was going to suggest heating the metal so it expands. Decent minds think alike 😜

author: @murb2586
text:
snap on tools has something called the "mini-ductor": induction bolt-heater.  the point of which is to selectively heat a bolt or nut without heating the surroundin metal or material too much as with a torch.  its $700.00, but i figured who could rig up one on the cheap better than you, nobody i know.  
just thought id throw that out there if it helps
huge thanks as always and keep up the good work Robert.

author: @goat-shaw
text: one of them things you see and say ofc why didnt i think of this first

author: @creepingjesus
text: Had to do the same myself on my bike last year. It nearly came down to buying a balljoint fork, cos there was no way I was paying a fortune for 'specialist' bike tools. They are frankly ridiculous: the exact same spanner is £7 or £15 depending on whether you buy it as a car fan hub tool, or a bike bottom bracket tool.

author: @totherarf
text: I would have thought that with your welding skills and your supply of nuts, bolts and stuff you would have cobbled together a puller for that task!

author: @daves_channel1491
text: Excellent, I am thinking of tank tracks and getting the split pin out, we never had heat to apply to them.  Good job!

author: @chuxxsss
text: Heat then freeze spray mate. We use to use it on tanks.

author: @lektwik
text: This works for disassembly of junk bikes. Not for repairs on something to be ridden. The Park tool is less than $20.

author: @superrodder2002
text: Working on cars in the shop and I may do that a half dozen times per day

author: @Velocipedium
text:
I use a car balljoint splitter, the fork type, between crank and bottom bracket shell.
The scissor type balljoint splitter is brilliant for the old style cotter pins, too.
I York’s bicycle recycling project, we used these methods literally thousands of times.

replies.text: I could - but this is for folks without a lathe

author: @William_Hada
text: Brilliant solution! Heat also works great on stubborn nuts and bolts.

author: @T.Cun.
text: When starting any mechanical endeavour always be mindful that, the right tool for the job is equal and opposite of the right tool at the job.

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: It's too bad I don't live anywhere near you or I'd just loan you my bike crank tool until I need it again.

author: @zylascope
text: A pain alright. I used a crank puller tool to get the crank arm off my e-bike recently and it stripped the thread in the crank arm. Thanks for sharing!

author: @burnroe6611
text: You can use any crank puller .i actually have a home made one there easy to make to

replies.author: @SlyerFox666
replies.text: True but you can buy one for a couple of quid off of eBay too.

replies.author: @scotttovey
@SlyerFox666
replies.text: @scotttovey Yea true true, mind you for a couple of quid, the time it takes plus the offset of the costs of running the lathe an tooling, lighting, heating an in my case risk of having an accident 😂 I just don't see the point these days, I bought a cheapy one well over ten years ago an had to use it about a year ago an still going strong.  Mind you I wouldn't be surprised if you could find a bolt with the right sized OD thread you could probably do it with a drill an a tap so maybe it's still worth it if your getting enough use out of it. I guess it just comes down to how much you value your time.
